coredata.dc:title: Fabrication of bismuth ferrite/graphitic carbon nitride/N-doped graphene quantum dots composite for high performance supercapacitors

coredata.dc:description: Supercapacitors are potential energy storage devices with a broad range of applications. In this study, we are investigating a bismuth ferrite/graphitic carbon nitride/N-doped graphene quantum dots composite as an electrode material for supercapacitor applications. XRD patterns of the composite exhibit the different crystalline phases of the individual component and confirm the rhombohedral structure of the composite. The wafer-like structure of bismuth ferrite is produced via hydrothermal technique supported on 2D structures viz. graphitic carbon nitride and N-doped graphene quantum dots. Compared to bismuth ferrite and bismuth ferrite/graphitic carbon nitride (g-CN) binary composite, the bismuth ferrite/g-CN/N-doped graphene quantum dots demonstrates a superior specific capacitance of 1472 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 current density. After 3000 charging-discharging cycles, the device maintains its cycling stability with 87% capacitance retention. A supercapacitor device is assembled utilizing bismuth ferrite/graphitic carbon nitride/N-doped graphene quantum dots and activated carbon as electrodes. This device shows a significantly improved performance with an energy density of 53.1 Wh kg−1 and a power density of 705.4 W kg−1. As a result, the composite electrode developed in this study is proved to be a potential electrode material for high-performance energy storage devices.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Supercapacitors are potential energy storage devices with a broad range of applications. In this study, we are investigating a bismuth ferrite/graphitic carbon nitride/N-doped graphene quantum dots composite as an electrode material for supercapacitor applications. XRD patterns of the composite exhibit the different crystalline phases of the individual component and confirm the rhombohedral structure of the composite. The wafer-like structure of bismuth ferrite is produced via hydrothermal technique supported on 2D structures viz. graphitic carbon nitride and N-doped graphene quantum dots. Compared to bismuth ferrite and bismuth ferrite/graphitic carbon nitride (g-CN) binary composite, the bismuth ferrite/g-CN/N-doped graphene quantum dots demonstrates a superior specific capacitance of 1472 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 current density. After 3000 charging-discharging cycles, the device maintains its cycling stability with 87% capacitance retention. A supercapacitor device is assembled utilizing bismuth ferrite/graphitic carbon nitride/N-doped graphene quantum dots and activated carbon as electrodes. This device shows a significantly improved performance with an energy density of 53.1 Wh kg−1 and a power density of 705.4 W kg−1. As a result, the composite electrode developed in this study is proved to be a potential electrode material for high-performance energy storage devices.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Supercapacitors (SCs) or electrochemical supercapacitors (ECs) are electrochemical energy storage devices and have potential applications in different forms like wearable electronics, portable-electronic systems, hybrid electric vehicles, and high-temperature rechargeable devices [1]. Since the charge is stored at the electrodes’ surface, SCs have higher cycle stability and quicker charging/discharging rates than batteries. The use of SCs in energy storage applications can help in developing devices to meet the needs necessitated by the population increase and environmental pollution. The electrolyte, electrode material, and cell design are chosen to optimize the enhanced performance of SCs [2–5]. Since the nature of the electrode material determines its capacitance, current research has focused on either developing novel electrode materials or modifying existing electrode materials to enhance their capacitances.

The charge transfer mechanism governs both main classes of supercapacitors, namely, electrochemical double-layer capacitors (EDLC) and pseudocapacitors. In pseudocapacitors, the fundamental charge storage mechanism is the faradaic redox processes that govern the oxidation states, whereas, in an EDLC, the storage of charge occurs electrostatically at the electrode and electrolyte interface. Electrodes formed of metal oxides have a higher storage capacity and energy density, but they have a lesser storage period leading to a shorter lifespan. The rate capability of EDLCs is high, whereas the energy density is low. It is difficult to increase the energy density of a SC without sacrificing its power density or rate capabilities. Several studies based on pseudocapacitors using ferrites [6], metal oxides such as Mn2O [7], thin films [8], carbon-containing substrates, etc., have been reported. According to the literature, adding carbonaceous elements to metal oxides, such as carbon nanotubes [9] and graphene [10], increases the composite materials's electrical conductivity and mechanical strength, which in turn will enhance their potential towards various energy applications [11–14]. Nitrogen functionalization is a feasible strategy to improve the characteristics of graphene-based materials. Further, nitrogen doping can increase the capacitative behavior and overall electrical conductivity of carbon materials [15]. The doped nitrogen functional groups accelerate the surface redox processes, which determine pseudocapacitance. Because of its acceptable band gap of roughly 2.9 eV, graphitic carbon nitride (g-CN) has sparked significant scientific interest in photocatalysis [16]. However, its prospective use in energy storage devices as an electrode material has received little attention from researchers. Because g-CN and graphene have comparable graphitic microstructures and g-CN has a high nitrogen functional group loading, altering graphene with g-CN is thought to be a potentially useful method of increasing the performance of graphene-based SCs [17].

The perovskite form of bismuth ferrite (BF), is a well-known multiferroic material with numerous applications, including photovoltaics, sensors, memory storage, and spintronics [18,19]. There have been very few reports on bismuth ferrite electrodes for supercapacitor applications till date [10,20–23]. In an aqueous NaOH electrolyte, Lokhande et al. [24] investigated thin films of perovskite bismuth ferrite for SC, and demonstrated a specific capacitance (Csp) of 81 F g−1. Modified bismuth ferrite material with different carbon substrates and TiO2 show considerable specific capacitance and stability. Additionally, although the application as an electroactive species of nanostructured BiFeO3 is not well established, just a few researchers have reported on its electrochemical performance. Here we have included some of the interesting works reported recently. Maswari and co-workers explored the electrochemical behavior of BiFeO3 with N doped carbon, where it showed a specific capacitance of 811 F g−1 [25]. The pseudocapacitor electrode made of BiFeO3 nanoflake was prepared by Pan and co-workers where it showed a capacitance up to 456 F g−1 [26]. Calcium doped BiFeO3 designed by Ma and Co-workers displayed a maximum capacitance of 305 F g−1 [27]. However, their results are not good enough to be treated as a supercapacitor [23]. Consequently, efforts have been made to improve the electrochemical performance of BiFeO3. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are nanostructures made up of graphene fragments. Biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity, and solubility in different solvents are some of the desirable properties; besides these, GQDs can be functionalized at the edges and are small enough to display fluorescence, exciton confinement, and quantum size effect. These properties have enabled several applications of GQDs like optoelectronics, fuel cells, photocatalysis, and sensors, to name but a few [28,29]. Doping GQDs with nitrogen can improve the reactivity and efficacy of the catalyst, notably in SCs applications [30–32].

The objective of this study is to prepare bismuth ferrite/g-CN/N-doped graphene quantum dots ternary (BGD) composite. To our understanding, this composite is reporting for the first time, utilizing hydrothermal method. The prepared BGD composite exhibited a high specific capacitance and remarkable stability, demonstrating its appropriateness for SCs applications as an electrode material. Here, g-CN functions as a link for the transmission of electrons among the wafer-like BF nanoparticles and the layered N-doped GQDs, thus improving the supercapacitor performance.

Materials utilized for this work, namely, bismuth nitrate (98%), ferric nitrate (98%), potassium hydroxide (98%), melamine (98%), citric acid (99%), ethylenediamine (98%), N-methylpyrrolidone (NMP) (99%), carbon black (98%), and polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) (99%) were of analytical grade (A.R). These were purchased from S.D. Fine Chemicals/Sigma Aldrich.

Fe(NO3)3⋅9H2O and Bi(NO3)3⋅5H2O in the 2:1 ratio were dissolved in KOH, and the resultant mixture was dissolved under magnetic stirring to get a homogeneous solution for 1 h. The obtained reaction solution was transferred to a Teflon-coated autoclave and kept at 200 °C for 6 h [33]. Finally, the obtained solution after filtration was calcined at 400 °C for 2 h to remove other impurities and other volatile substances in order to get pure BiFeO3 (BF). A crucible with melamine was heated at 550 °C for 4 h, to get the yellow colored g-CN and used for the subsequent preparation steps after grinding it to a uniform size [16].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of N-doped graphene quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: Citric acid (1 g) and ethylenediamine (0.4 mL) were added to 15 mL distilled water in a beaker and stirred for 1 h using magnetic stirring. This solution was autoclaved for 6 h at 180 °C in a muffle furnace. The obtained NGQDs solution was used for further reactions [29].

BF (1 g) and g-CN (0.5 g) were taken in an agate mortar and mixed thoroughly for 15 min. The mixture obtained was calcined for 4 h at 400 °C to form BiFeO3/g-CN (BG). This was used in the later steps required for the preparation of ternary composite.

1 g of BG solution in water and NGQDs (5 mL) was stirred for 2 h. The subsequent solution was transferred to an autoclave lined with Teflon and kept in a muffle furnace for about 6 h at 200 °C. It was filtered and washed with ethanol to get a brown powder of BGD (Fig. 1 ).

Bruker AXS Kappa Apex X-ray diffractometer (XRD) with CuKα (λ = 1.5418 Å) radiation in the 2θ range of 5–80° was used to examine the phase and crystal structure of the catalyst. Functional group determination was carried out with FTIR (Alpha-T ATR-FTIR Bruker) in 400–4000 cm−1 in transmittance mode. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was done using Thermal Analyzer STA 6000 PerkinElmer. The elemental composition and surface morphology were examined with the help of Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) and Energy Dispersive Analysis (EDS) (Quanta LV/ESEM). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy analysis (XPS) and High Resolution-Transmission Electron Microscopy (HRTEM) analysis were done using PHI VersaProbe III Thermo-scientific and (JEOL Japan, JEM-2100 Plus, respectively.

The working electrode of BF, BG, and BGD samples was designed by blending electro-active material, PVDF as a binder, and carbon black in the 80:10:10 ratio. NMP was the solvent for the preparation of slurry paste. The Ni-foam was cleaned using concentrated HCl solution under sonication to avoid the formation of NiO layer over Ni foam. The prepared slurry was then coated on Ni-foam within a specific area (1 cm × 1 cm). Finally, the remaining solvent was evaporated by drying the working electrode at 80 °C for 4 h in a hot air oven. To know more about the electrochemical performances towards the supercapacitor applications, cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) analysis was carried out using a CH electrochemical workstation (CHI 660C, USA) in a three-electrode cell setup in 2 M KOH electrolyte. The working, counter, and reference electrodes were the prepared composite coated on nickel foam, platinum wire, and Ag/AgCl, respectively. The amount of the active material used to coat the current collector is ∼0.8 mg cm−2.

The electrochemical studies of the BGD composite were assessed using CV, GCD, EIS, and cyclic stability experiments. The equation used for evaluating the specific capacitance from the GCD curve is given by Eq. (1). Asymmetric type supercapacitance (SC) device was fabricated using BGD and activated carbon as electrodes. 2 M KOH and Polypropylene film (Celgard, 2400) were utilized as electrolytes and separators. Owing to the high mobility of the OH− anions in water and their extremely smaller sizes, KOH has a very high ionic conductivity. In addition, the concentration is increased to 2 M in order to raise the ion count and, consequently, the electrolyte's ionic conductivity. These solutions are, therefore, almost as conductive as those of acids, which are commonly employed for supercapacitor applications [34]. Eq. (2) was used for calculating energy density, and Eq. (3) for power density of BGD.

here, Csp denotes the specific capacitance (F g−1), I, Δt, m, ΔV represent current in A g−1, difference in charge-discharging time in seconds, mass of the coated catalyst in mg cm−2, and the potential window, respectively.

Crystalline structure and phase purity of the synthesized nanocomposite was initially investigated using XRD analysis. The ternary and binary composite shows (Fig. 2 a) the peaks of pure BF samples indexed to a rhombohedral lattice (JCPDS card No. 20–0169), and the preferential growth of BF was oriented along the (110) plane. The diffraction peaks corresponding to (012), (104), (110), (006), (202), (024), (116), and (214) at 2θ values 22.3°, 31.5°, 31.7°, 38.6°, 39.1°, 45.4°, 51.0°, and 56.7° respectively confirm the rhombohedral structure of the composites. In the XRD pattern, the decrease in intensity of the BG and BGD suggests the formation of composite as the BF has been replaced with the g-CN and NGQDs. Comparable remarks were previously stated by Wang et al. for Bi2WO6/BiFeO3/g-CN composite [35]. NGQDs show major diffraction peaks at 2θ = 24.9° corresponding to (002), while g-CN generally shows a peak at 13.2° for (200) plane and 27.3° for (100) [36] (Fig. S1). Additionally, no noticeable diffraction peaks were observed for g-CN and NGQDs, which can be attributed to the smaller quantities present. Similar conditions have been noticed in the earlier reports [35,37].

Further, Debye–Scherrer formula [38] was used to calculate the crystallite size from the XRD analysis. The D value is obtained from the FWHM of the high intense XRD peak (2θ = 31.9°) and observed to be 11.56, 10.93 and 15.84 nm for BF, BG, and BGD samples, respectively. Further, the dislocation density can be obtained by taking the inverse of the square of the crystallite size [39], which is determined using Eq. (4) and is given in Table 1 . BGD has a higher crystallite size and lower dislocation density.

The prepared samples' FTIR spectra (Fig. 2b) reveal typical peaks at 520 and 438 cm−1, indicating bismuth ferrite formation [33]. The stretching vibration of CO is represented by the band at 1620 cm−1, and the stretching vibration of aromatic CC is shown by the multiple bands at 1540 cm−1, and the bending vibration of =C–H is represented by the bands at 675 to 1000 cm−1 [40]. The existence of aromatic CN/C–N of g-CN is attributed to the peaks detected near 3120 cm−1 [41] for N–H stretching and the peaks around 1200 - 1700 cm−1 for g-CN stretching. The existence of all these peaks in BGD's FTIR spectrum supports the composite's ternary composition.

Fig. 3 illustrates the SEM and EDS analysis of BF, BG, and BGD samples. BF sample shows a wafer-like structure, while the addition of g-CN to the sample gives a similar wafer-like morphology along with spindle-like structures. SEM image of BGD reveals a flake-like structure having porous nature with wafer and spindle structures in it. This type of structure enhances ion access, facilitates ion transport, and hence favours improved supercapacitor performance [42,43]. The NGQDs are not observed in SEM images because of their small size.

Fig. 3 illustrates the EDS reports for BF, BG, and BGD composite samples, which confirm the formation and composition of elements in the synthesized samples. The components are clearly scattered consistently in the BGD composite, implying that both the BF and NGQDs are well-formed on the surface of g-CN. The EDS study confirms that g-CN is consistently distributed, which is in line with the SEM results. Table 2 demonstrates the composition of various elements contained in the samples BF, BG, and BGD based on EDS data. Furthermore, the elements like Bi, Fe, O, C, and N in the BGD composite samples were confirmed by the EDS spectrum, which revealed no other impurities. Furthermore, the inclusion of NGQDs is confirmed by an increase in the weight % of C and N in BGD.

The microstructures of 2D bulk g-CN, BiFeO3, and NGQDs were revealed by HR-TEM analysis. The TEM image (Fig. 4 a) of bulk g-CN with BF and NGQDs demonstrates that the bulk material is formed with a large number of randomly oriented layers covering each other, as seen by the darker region of the image, and Fig. 4(b and c) depicts the bismuth ferrite and tiny graphene quantum dot structures distributed on the g-CN surface. SAED pattern (Fig. 4d) displays the crystalline nature of the prepared composite, and the particles are seen to be well ordered.

TGA analysis of the prepared samples was carried out from 25 to 800 °C while heating at 30 °C/min under a nitrogen atmosphere. Fig. S2 shows the TGA results of BF, BG, and BGD samples; in which BF is stable till 800 °C without any weight loss. From our previous studies, it is clear that g-CN has distinct regions where it loses weight [44]. The initial weight loss from 100 to 400 °C is due to surface adsorbed H2O, while the second weight-loss region 550–750 °C is ascribed to the decomposition of the g-CN. BGD sample has a similar decomposition pattern, beginning at 500 °C and complete elimination of g-CN occurring at 590 °C, indicating the inclusion of g-CN in the binary composite. Further increase in temperature up to 800 °C showed no loss of mass, indicating no further disintegration took place. The BGD TGA curve displays three weight-loss areas. The elimination of water, hydroxyl, carboxyl, and amide groups results in a continuous weight loss area up to 300 °C. The breakdown from 300 to 550 °C explains the elimination of phenyl groups. The disruption of g-CN is responsible for the third weight loss area. The decomposition of g-CN is delayed to 550 °C in this case. At 700 °C, the pyridine and pyrrolidine groups in NGQDs decompose, but no further deterioration occurs until 800 °C [45].

The chemical states and surface element compositions of the elements in the sample were investigated using XPS spectra of the BGD sample. The binding energies of Bi are 164.16 eV and 158.81 eV (Fig. 5 a), which correspond to the Bi 4f5/2 and Bi 4f7/2, respectively, which are characteristic of the Bi3+ atom found in BiFeO3 [35,46]. The C–N bond in g-CN [47], NGQDs, and CO in NGQDs have peaks at 284.97 eV and 288.16 eV [48], respectively (Fig. 5b). Iron (Fe) peaks at 711.34 eV and 722.54 eV are also aligned to Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2, respectively, showing that BiFeO3 contains Fe3+ [35,46] (Fig. 5c). The peak at 398.84 eV in the N 1s spectrum represents the N–C bond [49,50] (Fig. 5d). In addition, the binding energy of O 1s at 530.03 eV denotes the crystal lattice oxygen [51] (Fig. 5e). Furthermore, the crystal lattice oxygen binding energy of O 1s is 530.03 eV. (Fig. 5e). The existence of g-CN and NGQDs can be confirmed further using C 1s and N 1s spectra. Fig. 5f depicts the survey spectrum, which shows the composition of elements present in the sample. The presence of g-CN and NGQDs in the as-prepared material was confirmed by XPS analysis, which served to illustrate the chemical states and surface elemental composition in BGD.

Cyclic voltammetry (CV) is a primary analytical tool to identify the electrochemical nature of the prepared material. We have carried out CV analysis for the samples BF, BG, and BGD composite between the scan rate of 2–200 mV s−1 with −0.3 to 0.4 V potential window (Fig. 6 a). These CV curves illustrate the presence of distinct peaks of oxidation/reduction, inferring the influence of Faradaic reactions at the electrode/electrolyte interface. The broadening of loop area of BGD composite compared to BF and BG specifies the enhanced capacitive behavior of the material. Furthermore, Fig. 6b displaying the CV curves at distinct scan rates of BGD demonstrate a change in potential of cathodic and anodic peaks to lower and higher potentials, respectively. At a lower scan rate, electrolyte ions are properly intercalated into the inner electrode surface, resulting in a higher capacitance. The mobility of the electrolyte ions at the higher scan rate was restricted by its surface, resulting in an increased reversible redox potential, and the outer surface of the electrode alone participated in the charge storage process [52].

GCD measurements were used to probe the capacitive behavior and charge/discharge performance of the prepared electrodes. The GCD measurements for BGD sample were performed between −0.3 and 0.4 V (potential window) at different 1–10 A g−1 current densities (CDs). Fig. 6c depicts the GCD profile of BF, BG, and BGD samples at a constant CD of 1 A g−1 with a capacitance value of 152.75, 941.4, and 1472 F g−1, respectively. It is worth stating that BGD electrode has a higher discharge time, which may be ascribed to the presence of g-CN and NGQDs in bismuth ferrite, which significantly increases the capacitance of the sample owing to its higher electronic conductivity of carbon based materials. Fig. 6d represents the GCD profiles of BGD composite electrode measured in the range of 1–10 A g−1 CDs. The obtained Csp values (using Eq. (1)) are found to be 1472, 1232, 1139, 1094, 1007, 735.8, 782.7, 731.2, 658, and 612.14 F g−1 for 1–10 A g−1 CDs, respectively. As the CD increases, there is a constant reduction in the Csp due to the limited access of electrolyte ions over the active electrode material. Parallel leakages that shorten the shelf-life are the cause of quick self-discharge. The primary contributors to parallel leakages are non-uniform charge uptake throughout the surface of electrode material pores, parasitic redox processes involving impurities like metals and oxygen groups, and the faradaic reaction of electrolyte impurities [53]. Further, Csp will be boosted by the addition of layered carbon-based materials having outstanding electrical conductivity, which will act as an electrical junction among the constituents. The nitrogen containing g-CN and NGQDs will play a vital role in improving the electrochemical properties of the BGD sample since the nitrogen doped graphene exhibit excellent electrical conductivity, porous nature, and high surface area. When related to other works reported, the current study demonstrates exceptional specific capacitance [10,21,24,54,55].

EIS analysis is a significant investigation used to examine the kinetics of charge transfer at the electrode/electrolyte interface, ion transport, and electrical conductance. Fig. 6e depicts the Nyquist plot and equivalent circuit (inset) of BF, BG, and electrodes. The Nyquist plot is used to examine four resistive parameters such as charge-transfer resistance (Rct), solution resistance (Rs), Warburg resistance (W), and equivalent series resistance (ESR). The Nyquist plot has three major regions: high, medium, and low frequencies. The semicircle appears in the plot's high-frequency zone, its junction with the 45° line appears around medium-frequency zone, and the inclined line appears near low-frequency zone. Rs and Rct signify the starting and ending points respectively of the semicircle, whereas W denotes the slope obtained from 45° inclined line. ESR measures the difference between the solution and charge transfer resistances (ESR = Rct - Rs). The Rs value of BGD sample obtained from the Nyquist plot is 1.684 Ω. This lower value of Rct is attributed to the higher capacitance of BGD sample. The Rct value obtained for the BGD is more than the BG sample. It could be the result of surface defects developed as a result of the hydrothermal procedure used to prepare the BGD composite [56]. The observed values for Rs, Rct, ESR, and W for the three samples are given in Table 3 . The Randles circuit used to fit the EIS data is presented as an inset of Fig. 6e.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability tests, power densities and energy of BGD electrode
ce:section.ce:para: The cycling stability of electrode materials is considered the most important property for commercialization. Fig. 6f depicts the cycling stability behavior of the composite electrode up to 3000 continuous charge-discharge cycles at 15 A g−1 CD was recorded. The cyclic stability of BGD reveals that after 3000 cycles, 87% of the original capacitance is preserved. The inclusion of g-CN and NGQDs with BiFeO3 promotes greater active cites for quicker mobility of ions among the electrode material and electrolyte, which in turn improves the cyclic characteristics of the BGD composite.

To evaluate the applicability of as synthesized BGD for supercapacitor device applications, we developed an asymmetric-type supercapacitor cell using activated carbon and BGD as electrodes. A schematic representation of the SC cell is presented in Fig. S3(a). The CV analysis from 25 to 100 mV s−1 scan rates of the fabricated BGD device is depicted in Fig. 7 a. The analysis was carried out in 2 M KOH electrolyte with 0–1.4 V potential window. From the graph (Fig. 7a), we can observe distinct reduction and oxidation peaks which attributes to the pseudocapacitive behavior of the material. According to GCD measurements, the projected specific capacitance of the device drops steadily as the CD increases, i.e., 195, 134, 119, 111.7, 83, 80, 78.05, 76.7, 69.6, and 67 F g−1 at 1-10 A g−1 CDs (Fig. 7b). Furthermore, the fabricated device exhibited specific energy of 53.1 Wh kg−1 and specific power of 705.4 W kg−1 at a CD of 1 A g−1. Further, a charged cell constructed using BGD could energize an LED, as shown in Fig. S3(b). Fig. 7c shows the Nyquist plots derived from EIS analysis of the device, and the Rct and Rs value of the BGD device was found to be 10.9 and 1.75 Ω, respectively. The cycling stability of the as-fabricated device at 10 A g−1 is depicted in Fig. 8 . After 3000 continuous cycles, the capacitance retention of the BGD || C asymmetric device is 78%. The constructed BGD device has a shorter discharge time than the charge time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of present work with reported literature
ce:section.ce:para: The current work was compared to the electrochemical performances of bismuth ferrite composites from the available literature. BiFeO3 thin films were prepared for carrying out supercapacitor application with Csp of 72.2 F g−1 at 1 A g−1 which can be ascribed to a lower contact angle of the film, and capacitance retention of 82.8% is observed after 1500 cycles [54]. Lokhande et al. [24] reported BiFeO3 thin films with Csp of 81 F g−1 at 20 mV s−1 where copper substrate was utilized as the current collector. BiFeO3/GO composite was used as the supercapacitor material in two different studies using stainless steel substrate in a two electrode system showing a Csp of 64 F g−1 at 20 mV s−1 with 95% capacitance retention after 2000 cycles [10] and 9 mF cm−2 at 10 mV s−1 having 95% of capacitance retention after 5000 cycles [57]. The capacitive behavior can be credited to the enhanced surface area after the incorporation of GO sheets. A composite of BiFeO3 and TiO2 nanotubes with remarkable surface area was synthesized with improved Csp of 350 F g−1 at 2.5 A g−1 with 92.5% capacitance retention after 1200 cycles [55]. Similarly, BiFeO3/N doped carbon composite demonstrated 811 F g−1 at 50 mV s−1 in three electrode systems [25]. Moitra et al. [58] presented a hydrothermal method for preparing BiFeO3/rGO, claiming the maximum Csp of 928.43 F g−1 at 5 A g−1 using Ni foam as a current collector. Table 4 gives a comparison of the current work with the existing literature. The newly prepared BiFeO3/g-CN/NGQDs synthesized using the hydrothermal technique has a better Csp of 1472 F g−1 at 1 A g−1. The BGD device fabricated has other attractive characteristics, including a specific energy of 53.1 Wh kg−1 and power of 705.4 W kg−1. The BGD electrode's maximum specific capacitance of 1472 F g−1 is greater than previously reported results for carbon containing asymmetric electrode materials.

A ternary composite of bismuth ferrite/g-CN/NGQDs, prepared via hydrothermal method followed by calcination as an electrode material, was evaluated for SCs applications. The present work illustrates the efficiency of the ternary composite as a pseudocapacitor material and is shown to be better than pure bismuth ferrite (BF) and bismuth ferrite/graphitic carbon nitride (BG) binary composite. The improved electrochemical performance of the BGD sample was evaluated using CV, GCD, and EIS measurements. The calculated specific capacitance of BF, BG, and BGD at 1 A g−1 was found to be 152.75, 941.4, and 1472 F g−1, respectively. Besides, excellent electrochemical properties of BGD sample exhibited outstanding cycling stability for 3000 cycles using GCD analysis, and capacitance retention is found to be 87%. These results demonstrate that the synergetic impact of various metal oxides with g-CN and NGQDs for high-performance pseudocapacitors is expected to be a promising way forward for its practical application.
